data.title: What's Behind The Pop In Meta Stock Today?
data.description:
Meta Platforms Inc (NASDAQ:META) stock is getting a boost on Monday. The company received a a flurry of positive analyst coverage.

  • Meta Platforms stock is showing upward movement. What’s driving META shares up?

Multiple Analysts Say It’s Time To Buy Meta Stock

Rothschild & Co's James Cordwell upgraded Meta from Neutral to Buy and hiked his price target to $900 from $740, arguing the stock has substantial room to run from current levels, Investing.com reported. The call comes despite concerns about Meta's rising spending.

Cordwell noted that CEO Mark Zuckerberg is in "founder mode," pushing aggressively into AI regardless of costs. Rothschild now expects Meta's total expenses in fiscal 2026 to reach $158.6 billion, well above consensus, and raised its capital‑expenditure forecast to $117.1 billion.

Still, Rothschild said that with shares around $650, the long‑term upside far outweighs near‑term risks.

Elsewhere on the Street, KeyBanc's Justin Patterson reiterated his Overweight rating on Meta, though he trimmed his price target to $835 from $875. Roth Capital's Rohit Kulkarni also provided a similar update on Monday, keeping a Buy rating on shares while lowering the target to $800 from $845.

Meta’s Momentum Builds

Meta is currently trading above its key short-term moving averages, indicating a bullish sentiment among traders. The stock is positioned 4.7% above its 20-day SMA and 5.4% above its 50-day SMA, suggesting that momentum is to the upside, although it is slightly below the 100-day and 200-day SMAs.

The RSI is at 56.89, which is considered neutral territory, indicating that while there is some buying momentum, the stock isn’t overbought, allowing for potential further upside without immediate risk of a pullback. Meanwhile, the MACD is above its signal line, which points to bullish momentum in the near term.

Key support is at $638.50, while resistance is set at $711.00. If the stock approaches the support level, traders should be cautious, as a breach could signal a trend reversal, while a breakout above resistance could lead to a new uptrend.

In December, a death cross occurred when the 50-day SMA crossed below the 200-day SMA. This crossover can often signal a shift in trend, and traders should watch for how the stock reacts in the coming weeks and months.

Over the past 12 months, Meta has seen a modest gain of 2.17%. This performance reflects a longer-term trend that has been relatively stable, but traders should remain vigilant for any signs of volatility or shifts in momentum.

Currently, the stock is positioned at 61.5% of its 52-week range, indicating it is closer to its highs than lows. This positioning suggests that while there is potential for further gains, traders should be aware of the resistance levels that could challenge upward movement.

META Price Action: Meta shares were up 2.05% at $672.28 at the time of publication on Monday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Kohlberg Flips The Switch On $2.4 Billion ENTRUST Sale
data.description:
Middle-market private equity firm Kohlberg has agreed to sell ENTRUST Solutions Group to Leidos Holdings Inc (NYSE:LDOS) for approximately $2.4 billion.

• Leidos Holdings shares are experiencing downward pressure. What’s driving LDOS stock lower?

The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, stated an announcement made by the firm.

Houlihan Lokey and Perella Weinberg Partners are serving as financial advisors, Ropes & Gray is serving as legal advisor, and KPMG is serving as accounting advisor to ENTRUST and Kohlberg in connection with the transaction.

"Our collaboration with Kohlberg and resulting scale has strengthened our ability to serve customers and will enable us to continue strengthening North America's critical power infrastructure for years to come," Adam Biggam, Entrust CEO said.

Kohlberg acquired ENTRUST, a consulting and engineering services platform, in 2019, with a minority recapitalization in 2023. During the last six years, ENTRUST has expanded its geographic reach, service capabilities and customer base through organic growth, strategic acquisitions and scaled workforce and operational infrastructure through the acquisition.

Kohlberg invests in health care and services companies and manages approximately $17 billion in assets as of Sept. 30, 2025.

Photo: PeopleImages via Shutterstock

data.title: If You Hate Inflation, Then You'll Love Stocks
data.description:
Stocks are considered an inflation hedge.

It makes sense when you think about it.

Consumers and businesses experience inflation through the higher prices of goods and services. And those goods and services are sold by companies. And many of those companies comprise the publicly traded corporations listed on the stock market.

These companies aren't always raising prices. But when they do, it's because they believe it'll support earnings growth. Often, the increase occurs in the context of passing on their own rising costs to their customers.

All this speaks to how companies have been dealing with tariffs. From Morgan Stanley's Jan. 13 research note addressing tariff costs:

US Corporates Are Leveraging Pricing Power — Bullish for Earnings and Supportive of the Labor Market

Hidden in the depths of the Q3 US GDP print was evidence that firms are now starting to raise prices. The data in the GDP release is corroborated by data from the National Federation of Independent Businesses. We believe that companies are raising prices to pass through tariff costs to consumers rather than absorb a hit to margins. We view this as bullish for corporate earnings, while also reducing downside risk to labor markets.

This is consistent with what companies had been saying for much of last year.* And analysts expect margins to expand this year and earnings to grow at a double-digit rate through at least 2027. Notably, earnings are the most important driver of stock prices.

This dynamic between inflation and stock prices is not new.

Last October, Deutsche Bank published a study on asset prices over the past 200 years. They considered the relationship over 25-year periods. From the report:

Nominal equity returns rise almost linearly with inflation, suggesting that equities serve as an effective inflation hedge—particularly relative to cash. However, real returns tend to decline slightly as inflation increases, indicating that equities perform best in lower-inflation environments. Over a 25-year horizon though, the difference in real returns between moderate deflation (around 0% to -2% inflation) and fairly strong inflation (around 8–9%) is relatively small; the sharp decline occurs only at very high inflation levels.

Therefore, if you expect a structurally higher inflation regime ahead, equities should remain a reasonable good nominal hedge over the long run, with real returns likely to stay relatively well protected.

In the context of stocks acting as a hedge against inflation, the key finding here is that nominal returns have moved "almost linearly with inflation." The fact that real returns (i.e., returns adjusted for inflation) have held up under most inflation scenarios is a bonus.

In the words of Ritholtz Wealth's Ben Carlson, it's a "pretty darn good long-term hedge to me."

The big picture 🖼️

Publicly traded companies are not charities.

Their approach to pricing — whether it's lowering them or raising them — is about maximizing earnings.

Sometimes that means raising prices on the goods and services they sell to you.

And you don't have to like it.

But if you are invested in the stock market, you have exposure to the side that's raising those prices.

A popular reaction to the claim that tariffs have boosted prices is to ask, "Then why hasn't inflation been higher?" While inflation rates have generally been steady, they remain above the Fed's 2% target rate. We have to consider the possibility that inflation rates would be much lower if not for tariffs.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Natural Gas Rallies 30%, Silver Jumps 13% To $116: What's Moving Markets Monday?
data.description:
U.S. natural gas prices extended a historic rally Monday, jumping more than 30% as Winter Storm Fern drove heating demand sharply higher and disrupted supply, delivering the largest weekly percentage gain ever for a Nymex front-month contract.

• Cloudflare stock is among today’s top performers. What’s behind NET gains?

The Henry Hub front-month contract now trades above $6 per million British thermal units. Just a week ago, prices hovered near $3, marking a roughly 125% surge in four sessions.

Dollar weakness added fuel to a broader commodity breakout. Gold rose 2.2% to $5,100. Silver surged 13% to $116 per ounce — on track for its best one-day surge since September 2008. This gain is also lifting its rolling 12-month gain to 266%, the strongest since 1980.

The greenback remained under pressure as the Japanese yen strengthened sharply, with speculators suspecting currency intervention occurred on Friday, potentially involving the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

Outside commodities, U.S. equities continued to grind higher. The S&P 500 now sits less than half a percentage point below record highs.

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 each gained 0.7%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.5%. The Russell 2000 lagged, ending flat.

Technology stocks outperformed on Monday. Memory-linked names, including Seagate Technology Holdings PLC (NASDAQ:STX), Western Digital Corp (NASDAQ:WDC) and SanDisk Corp. (NASDAQ:SNDK) extended gains amid ongoing supply disruptions in the DRAM market.

By contrast, Intel Corp (NASDAQ:INTC) slid 5%, following a 17% plunge on Friday after issuing disappointing guidance.

Mining stocks also pushed higher, supported by rising metal prices. Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX) gained 4.6%, while Newmont Corp. (NYSE:NEM) climbed 3.6%.

Monday’s Performance In Major U.S. Indices, ETFs

According to Benzinga Pro platform:

  • The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSE:VOO) rose 0.62% to $637.73.
  • The SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average (NYSE:DIA) rose 0.50% to $493.37.
  • The tech-heavy Invesco QQQ Trust Series (NASDAQ:QQQ) rose 0.64% to $626.73.
  • The iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSE:IWM) slipped 0.17% to $264.36.
  • The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLK) outperformed, up 1.0%; the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLY) lagged, down 0.5%.

Russell 1000's Top Gainers (Midday)

Russell 1000's Top Losers (Midday)

data.title: Silver Soars To Record Highs Amid Global Tensions
data.description:
iShares Silver Trust (NYSE:SLV) shares are jumping on Monday as silver prices break into new all‑time highs, fueled by rising geopolitical tensions and growing worries about global financial stability.

  • iShares Silver Trust shares are testing new highs. Why are SLV shares at highs?

The latest spark came over the weekend, when President Donald Trump warned he would slap a 100% tariff on Canada if it moves ahead with a new trade agreement with China. Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney recently traveled to Beijing and reached a preliminary deal that would cut tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles in exchange for reduced duties on Canadian canola.

China pushed back on Monday, saying its arrangements with Canada "are not targeted at any third party," and urged countries to approach international relations with a "win‑win, not zero‑sum" mindset, Reuters reported.

Another Government Shutdown?

The U.S. is once again on the brink of a government shutdown, barely two months after the record‑setting 43‑day closure in late 2025. Trump warned that "we're probably going to end up in another Democrat shutdown."

Tensions escalated following Senate Democrats' decision to block funding for the Department of Homeland Security after a fatal shooting in Minneapolis involving U.S. Border Patrol agents.

Prediction markets are now heavily pricing in a shutdown on Jan. 31. More than $8 million has been wagered on Kalshi's "Government shutdown on Saturday?" contract, with bettors assigning a roughly 77% probability that federal agencies will partially close.

Fed Meeting Adds Another Layer Of Uncertainty

The Federal Reserve meets this week and is widely expected to keep interest rates unchanged. But the meeting is being overshadowed by a Trump administration criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, cited Reuters. Trump has repeatedly pressured Powell to cut rates.

"We're seeing a wave of first‑time investors driving this surge in precious metals," said Adrian Ash, head of research at BullionVault. "Private investors across Asia and Europe are rushing to build their personal holdings of gold and silver."

SLV Price Action: iShares Silver Trust shares were up 12.86% at $104.71 at the time of publication on Monday. The stock is trading at a new 52-week high, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock
